1: wattāšar dəḇôrāh ûḇārāq bin-ʾăḇînoʿam bayyôm hahûʾ lēʾmōr — 'Then Deborah and Barak son of Abinoam sang on that day, saying.' The Song of Deborah is one of the oldest extant Hebrew poetic texts, displaying archaic linguistic features (archaic verb forms, rare vocabulary, paratactic syntax) that scholars date to around the 12th century BCE. šîr (H7891, to sing) — victory hymns after deliverance are a consistent pattern: Miriam's Song (Exod 15), the Song of Moses (Deut 32). The dual authorship — Deborah and Barak — reflects their joint role; the Song was evidently her composition performed together.
2: bipraʿ pərāʿôt bəyiśrāʾēl — One of the most debated lines in Judges. pāraʿ (H6544) means 'to let loose, to lead/be free.' The phrase may mean 'When leaders led in Israel' (NRSV, ESV — reading pərāʿôt as a verbal noun for leaders who step up) or 'when men were freely offered/volunteered' (KJV, NASB) or 'when locks of hair were let loose' (reading the Nazirite dedication of warriors as the context). The phrase sets the rhetorical tone: a moment of voluntary, spirited commitment to YHWH's cause. bārûk YHWH — bless YHWH: the Song opens and closes (v31) with YHWH's honor; the victory is his to be praised for.
3: šimʿû məlākîm haʾăzînû rōzənîm ʾānōkî laYHWH ʾānōkî ʾāšîr ʾăzammēr laYHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'Hear, O kings! Give ear, O rulers! I, to YHWH I will sing; I will make music to YHWH, the God of Israel.' The address to kings and rulers as the audience of the victory hymn is a bold theological claim: YHWH's triumph over Sisera is news the kings of the earth must hear. rōzen (H7336, ruler/prince — rare; occurs only 6 times) adds a second tier of leadership to the audience. The double ʾānōkî laYHWH (I, to YHWH) is emphatic dedication: this song is given TO YHWH, not merely about him.
4: YHWH bəṣēʾtkā miśśēʿîr — 'YHWH, when you went out from Seir' — the theophanic march-poem. This strophe (vv4-5) is closely parallel to Deuteronomy 33:2 and Psalms 68:8-9: YHWH appears marching from the southern wilderness (Seir, Paran, Sinai). The theophanic imagery includes earthquake (rāʿaš H7493, the earth quaked), melting mountains (nāzəlû, the mountains streamed), and storm: ʿāḇîm nāṭāpû mayim — 'the clouds dripped water.' The cosmological scope asserts YHWH as lord of all creation forces, not merely a tribal deity. These archaic theophany poems preserve the oldest Israelite traditions of YHWH's warrior-appearance from the southern mountains.
6: bîmê šamgar bin-ʿănāt bîmê yāʾēl ḥāḏəlû ʾoraḥôt — 'In the days of Shamgar son of Anath, in the days of Jael, the highways ceased.' ʾōraḥ (H734, road/path) + ḥāḏal (H2308, to cease/stop) describes a state of such insecurity that normal travel has stopped — the sign of complete social breakdown. The reference to pərāzôn (unwalled villages, H6520) in v7 and the absence of shields and spears among 40,000 (v8) confirms an Israel stripped of military capacity. This context makes the victory all the more remarkable: YHWH wins with no conventional army.
11: məqôl məḥaṣṣəṣîm bên mašʾabbôt šam yəṯannû ṣiḏqôt YHWH ṣiḏqōt pirzonô bəyiśrāʾēl — 'At the sound of those who divide flocks among the watering places, there they rehearse the righteous acts of YHWH, the righteous acts of his village warriors in Israel.' ṣiḏqāh (H6666, righteousness/righteous act/victory) — the 'righteous acts of YHWH' is a rich phrase: it encompasses both YHWH's covenant faithfulness and his saving interventions. The setting — watering holes where shepherds gather — suggests the victory story is transmitted in communal oral recitation. pārazon/pārāzî (v7, H6520/H6521 village-dwellers/open-land warriors) are the free farmers who volunteered, contrasting with professional armies.
20: min-šāmayim nilḥāmû hakkôkāḇîm mimməsilôtām nilḥāmû ʿim-sîsərāʾ — 'From heaven the stars fought, from their courses they fought against Sisera.' Stars as divine warriors fighting on YHWH's behalf is ancient cosmological language: the heavenly host (H6635 ṣāḇāʾ) is YHWH's army, and its deployment against Sisera means all of creation is arrayed against him. The verse may also encode meteorological reality — the heavenly disruption (storm, flooding of the Kishon, v21) that made the iron chariots useless. məsillâ (H4546, course/highway — their ordained paths) — the stars fight from their divinely appointed courses, implying cosmic order weaponized against the enemies of covenant.
21: naḥal qîšôn gərāpām naḥal qədûmîm naḥal qîšôn — 'The Kishon swept them away, the ancient river, the Kishon river.' qîšôn (H7028) is the river draining the Jezreel Valley toward the sea. gārap (H1640, to sweep away — only here and Psalms 40:3) describes a flash flood sweeping things away. qəḏûmîm (ancient/primeval, H6924) gives the river an age-old character — the Kishon has always been the place of divine victory. The flooding of the Kishon is the natural mechanism by which YHWH's battle began: iron chariot wheels mired in mud become a trap rather than an asset.
31: kēn yōḇdû kol-ʾōyəḇêkā YHWH wəʾōhăḇāyw kəṣēʾt haššemeš bigbûrātô — 'So shall all your enemies perish, O YHWH! But let those who love him be like the sun as it rises in its might.' The Song's closing benediction is structured as an antithetic parallelism: the fate of YHWH's enemies (perishing) vs. the fate of his lovers (radiant ascent). gəḇûrāh (H1369, might/strength/valor) applied to the rising sun is a striking metaphor: the sun at dawn rises with growing force, unchallenged. ʾōhăḇāyw (those who love him, H157) — covenant love language; in Deuteronomy 6:5 Israel is commanded to love YHWH with all their heart, soul, and strength. The covenant lovers will match the solar glorification at the end of history (cf. Matthew 13:43 — 'the righteous will shine like the sun in the kingdom of their Father').
5: hārîm nāzəlû mippənê YHWH zeh sînay mippənê YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — 'The mountains quaked before YHWH, even Sinai before YHWH, the God of Israel.' nāzal (H5140, to flow/drip/stream down) — the mountains do not merely shake but pour forth, a hyperbolic image of cosmic dissolution before the divine march. zeh sînay (this is Sinai / the one of Sinai) is the formulaic identifier appended to YHWH: he is the God whose definitive self-revelation was at Sinai. The Sinai theophany (Exod 19-20) is the template for all subsequent divine appearances — earthquake, fire, and storm. This verse anchors the present victory in the primal covenant-founding event.
7: ḥāḏəlû pərāzôn bəyiśrāʾēl ḥāḏēlû ʿaḏ šaqqamtî dəḇôrāh šaqqamtî ʾēm bəyiśrāʾēl — 'Village life ceased in Israel, it ceased until I, Deborah, arose — I arose as a mother in Israel.' pərāzôn (H6520, open-country villages/rural population) — the unfortified settlements are the vulnerable core of Israel's agrarian life; their cessation means the countryside has been abandoned to the enemy. ʾēm bəyiśrāʾēl (mother in Israel, H517) — the title is unique in the judges narratives; Deborah is not a 'father in Israel' (the male patriarchal title) but a mother — one who nourishes, gathers, protects. The maternal metaphor for covenant leadership anticipates Isaiah 66:13 ('As one whom his mother comforts, so I will comfort you') and Jesus's lament over Jerusalem (Matthew 23:37).
8: yiḇḥar ʾĕlōhîm ḥăḏāšîm ʾāz leḥem šəʿārîm māgēn wəʾim-yērāʾeh wārōmaḥ bəʾarbaʿîm ʾelep bəyiśrāʾēl — 'They chose new gods; then war was in the gates. Was a shield or spear seen among 40,000 in Israel?' The verse is among the most debated in the Song for its syntax and referent: the 'they' who chose new gods is Israel, and the consequence was war at the gates (the enemy was inside the defensive perimeter). The military disarmament — no shield (māgēn H4043) or spear (rōmaḥ H7420) among forty thousand — is the result of idolatry. When Israel abandons YHWH, YHWH allows the removal of the means of self-defense. The victory that follows will be unmistakably divine.
9: libbî ləḥôqəqê yiśrāʾēl hammitnaḏḏəḇîm bāʿām bārăkû YHWH — 'My heart is with the commanders (ḥōqēq) of Israel who offered themselves willingly (nāḏaḇ) among the people — bless YHWH.' ḥōqēq (H2710, one who inscribes/decrees/commands — from the root of engraving the law; used of the lawgiver's scepter in Genesis 49:10 and Numbers 21:18) — the word elevates the tribal commanders to quasi-legislative status: they are those who decree and lead. hitnaddēḇ (H5068, to volunteer/offer oneself freely) is the key virtue celebrated in the Song: the willing offering of oneself in response to covenant crisis, parallel to the freewill offerings of the tabernacle (Exodus 35:21-29).
10: rōḵəḇê ʾătōnôt ṣəḥōrôt yōšəḇê ʿal-miḏîn wəhōlăkê ʿal-dereḵ śîḥû — 'You who ride on tawny donkeys, you who sit on rich carpets, and you who walk by the road — consider!' ʾătōn ṣəḥōrāh (H860, H6715 — tawny/white she-donkey) is the mount of dignity in the ancient Near East (cf. Judges 10:4; Judges 12:14 — sons who rode on seventy donkeys; Zechariah 9:9 — the king comes on a donkey). The triad — riders of distinction, those at ease, travelers on the road — represents all of Israelite society being summoned to attend and recount the victory. śîḥû (H7878, to muse/meditate/speak) — the root suggests both inner meditation and outward declaration of YHWH's acts.
12: ʿûrî ʿûrî dəḇôrāh ʿûrî ʿûrî daббərî-šîr qûm bārāq ûšəḇeh šiḇyəkā bin-ʾăḇînoʿam — 'Awake, awake, Deborah! Awake, awake, strike up a song! Rise up, Barak, and lead away your captives, son of Abinoam.' The fourfold ʿûrî (H5782, awake!/rouse yourself!) is a battle cry and a summons to prophetic speech — the same root that opens the Servant's ear in Isaiah 50:4 ('He awakens my ear morning by morning'). The double imperative — awake to song, rise to captives — links prophetic proclamation with military reality. šəḇeh šiḇyəkā (H7617/H7628, lead away your captives) is the language of post-battle spoil-taking, used of YHWH's own cosmic victories (Psalms 68:18).
13: ʾāz yāraḏ śārîṭ laʾaddîrîm ʿam YHWH yāraḏ lî baggibborîm — 'Then down marched the remnant of the noble; the people of YHWH marched down for me among the mighty.' śārîṭ is textually uncertain (some read śārîd H8300, survivor/remnant); the meaning is those who remain/the remnant who stepped up. ʾaddîr (H117, noble/mighty — used of YHWH's own majesty in Psalms 8:1) applied to the volunteers exalts the tribal warriors to a regal dignity. ʿam YHWH (people of YHWH) — the designation that marks the fighters as covenant participants, not merely tribal levies. Their descent into battle is YHWH's own descent.
14: minnî ʾeprayim šārāšām bāʿămālēq ʾaḥărêkā ḇinyāmîn bəʿămmêkā — 'From Ephraim their root was in Amalek; after you, Benjamin, with your clans.' The tribal roll call of participation/non-participation is the Song's sharpest rhetorical device: praise for those who came, oblique rebuke for those who did not. Ephraim's root 'in Amalek' is enigmatic — possibly referring to a region called Amalek within Ephraim's territory (cf. Judges 12:15, 'the hill country of the Amalekites'). Benjamin 'after you' places the southern tribe behind Ephraim in the march order. Machir (representing Manasseh) and Zebulun follow — tribal volunteers honored by name in the divine record.
15: wəśārāyw byiśśāśkār ʿim-dəḇôrāh wəyiśśāśkār kēn ḇārāq bāʿēmeq šullaḥ bəraglāyw — 'The commanders of Issachar came with Deborah; and Issachar — so faithful to Barak; into the valley they rushed at his feet.' šālaḥ bəraglāyw (sent/rushed at his feet — idiom for following energetically) — Issachar's loyalty is marked by the pun: they rushed at Barak's feet (raglāyw) just as the troops followed Barak 'at his feet' (v10). bip̄laggôt rəʾûḇēn gədôlîm ḥiqqê-lēḇ — 'Among the clans of Reuben there were great searchings of heart' — the first note of rebuke; Reuben deliberated but did not come.
16: lāmmāh yāšaḇtā bên hammišpətāyim lišmōaʿ šəriqôt ʿăḏārîm — 'Why did you sit among the sheepfolds, listening to the piping for the flocks?' The question directed at Reuben is rhetorical and devastating: while the battle raged, Reuben stayed home to tend sheep. mišpetayim (H4942, sheepfolds/saddlebags — dual form, possibly meaning the two folds) and šərîqôt ʿăḏārîm (the piping/whistling for the flocks — the shepherd's call) paint a picture of pastoral complacency while covenant brothers fought for survival. lipəlaggôt rəʾûḇēn gədôlîm ḥiqqrê-lēḇ — 'Among the clans of Reuben there were great searchings (ḥeqer H2714, deep searchings/investigations) of heart (lēḇ)' — not courage but indecision, the deep deliberation that leads to inaction.
17: gilʿāḏ bəʿēḇer hayyardēn šākān wəḏān lāmmāh yāgûr ʾŏniyyôt ʾāšēr yāšaḇ ləḥôp hayyammîm ûʿal mipərāṣāyw yiškôn — 'Gilead stayed beyond the Jordan; Dan, why did he stay beside the ships? Asher sat still at the coast of the sea, settling beside his inlets.' Gilead (Gad + half-Manasseh east of Jordan), Dan, and Asher are rebuked for geographic/economic calculations overriding covenant solidarity. Dan's association with ships (ʾŏniyyôt H591) implies a maritime trade connection — possibly Dan had settled in coastal areas where commercial interests trumped military ones. Asher's sitting at the sea-coast (ḥôp hayyām) confirms a similar maritime orientation. The contrast with Zebulun and Naphtali (v18) is total.
18: zəḇûlun ʿam ḥērēp napšô lāmût wənaptālî ʿal mərômê śāḏeh — 'Zebulun is a people who risked their lives to the point of death; Naphtali also, on the heights of the field.' ḥārap (H2778, to reproach/defy/expose to contempt — here used in the sense of despising one's own life, giving it away) — Zebulun 'reproached' or 'exposed' their lives to death (lāmût). The idiom means they staked their lives recklessly. Naphtali on the 'heights of the field' (mərômê śāḏeh) stood exposed on the high ground — the position of maximum visibility and maximum vulnerability. The willingness to die for YHWH's cause is the Song's highest honor.
19: bāʾû məlākîm nilḥāmû ʾāz nilḥəmû malkê kənaʿan bətaʿnak ʿal-mê məgiddô beṣaʿ kesef lōʾ lāqāḥû — 'Kings came, they fought; then the kings of Canaan fought at Taanach by the waters of Megiddo; they took no plunder of silver.' The battle is elevated to a royal-level conflict — the Canaanite coalition represents the full weight of regional power. Taanach and Megiddo are the two great fortress cities flanking the Jezreel Valley's narrowest point, the strategic chokehold of the region. beṣaʿ kesef lōʾ lāqāḥû — 'no plunder of silver did they take' — the expected outcome of victory (plunder) was reversed: the intended plunderers (the kings) took nothing, because YHWH routed them before they could collect.
22: ʾāz hālamû ʿiqqəḇê-sûs midahărôt daharôt ʾabbîrāyw — 'Then loud beat the horses' hoofs with the galloping, galloping of his chargers.' dāhar (H1725, to gallop — onomatopoetic; the doubling daharôt daharôt mimics the drumming of hoofs) captures the sound of the routing cavalry. ʿiqqēḇ (H6119, heel/footprint/hoof-beat) — the stamping hoofs of panicking horses encode the terror of the routed army. The phonetic texture of this verse — alliterative, percussive — is among the finest examples of archaic Hebrew sound-poetry, imitating the very battle sounds it describes.
23: ʾôrû mērôz ʾāmar malʾak YHWH ʾôrû ʾārôr yōšəḇêhā kî lōʾ-ḇāʾû ləʿezrat YHWH ləʿezrat YHWH baggibborîm — 'Curse Meroz, says the angel of YHWH, curse its inhabitants bitterly, because they did not come to the help of YHWH, to the help of YHWH against the mighty.' ārûr (H779, curse — the covenant curse, the opposite of bārûk) pronounced through the divine messenger is the strongest possible negative. Meroz is the only place cursed by name; its location is unknown, which has led some to see it as a deliberate mystery — any community that refuses YHWH's call is Meroz. ʿezrat YHWH — 'to the help of YHWH' — a striking reversal: YHWH does not need human help, but covenant participation in YHWH's battle is how faithfulness is demonstrated.
24: təḇōrak minnāšîm yāʾēl ʾēšet ḥeḇer haqqênî minnāšîm bāʾōhel təḇōrāk — 'Most blessed of women be Jael, the wife of Heber the Kenite — of tent-dwelling women most blessed.' bārak (H1288, to bless) in the Piel — the intensive blessing formula reserved for those who act decisively for YHWH's cause. 'Most blessed of women' (minnāšîm — the comparative partitive: more blessed than all women) anticipates the same formula used of Mary in Luke 1:42 ('Blessed are you among women'). Both Jael and Mary are women through whom the decisive blow against the enemy comes — Jael with the tent peg, Mary by bearing the one who would crush the serpent's head.
25: mayim šāʾal ḥālāḇ nāṯānāh bəsēpel ʾaddîrîm hiqrîḇāh ḥemʾāh — 'He asked for water, she gave him milk; in a lordly bowl she brought him curds (ḥemʾāh).' ḥemʾāh (H2529, cream/curds/butter — the best of milk products) in a sēpel ʾaddîrîm (H5602 H117, a basin of nobles/magnificent bowl) — she treated him like a honored guest. The irony is pointed: the finest hospitality — better than he asked for, served in a royal vessel — precedes his death. The Song's version of the killing (vv26-27) is more elaborate than the prose account, showing Jael as a skilled warrior rather than an opportunistic killer.
26: yāḏāh layyāṯēḏ tišlaḥnāh wîmînāh ləhalmût ʿămēlîm — 'Her hand she sent to the tent peg, her right hand to the workmen's mallet.' yāṯēḏ (H3489, tent peg) and halmût (H1989, mallet — related to hāmam to strike/shatter) — the domestic tools recast as weapons. The Song's slow, deliberate enumeration — hand to peg, right hand to mallet, she hammered, she struck, she pierced — is designed to make the audience dwell on each action. wəhālaməṯāh sîsərāʾ māḥăqāh rōʾšô ûmāḥăṣāh wəḥālǝpāh raqqātô — she hammered Sisera, she crushed his head, she shattered and pierced his temple. The verbs pile up: māḥaq (H4272, to shatter), māḥaṣ (H4272, to smite/wound through), ḥālap (H2498, to pierce through).
27: bên raglêhā kāraʿ nāpal šāḵaḇ bên raglêhā kāraʿ nāpal baʾăšer kāraʿ šām nāpal šāḏûḏ — 'Between her feet he sank, he fell, he lay; between her feet he sank, he fell; where he sank, there he fell — destroyed.' The triple repetition (kāraʿ nāpal — sank, fell) is the Song's triumphant cadence over the dead commander. šāḏûḏ (H7703, destroyed/devastated) — the final word tolls like a bell. The three-beat descent — sank, fell, lay still — may deliberately mirror the fall of an idol: the great and feared becomes prostrate before a woman of God. Psalms 20:8 uses the same verbs: 'They collapse and fall, but we rise and stand upright.'
28: bəʿaḏ hallôn nišqǝpāh wattəyabbēb ʾēm sîsərāʾ bəʿaḏ hāʾešnāb — 'Out of the window she peered, the mother of Sisera wailed through the lattice.' The scene shifts to Sisera's home — his mother watching and waiting. nišqǝpāh (H8259, to peer/look out) and yābab (H2980, to wail/cry out) paint the waiting woman's grief beginning even before she knows the outcome. The window-and-lattice scene (ʾešnāb H822, a latticed window) is an image from Canaanite iconography — the 'woman in the window' is a motif found on carved ivories from Megiddo and other sites, depicting a noble woman watching for a lover's return. Here the beloved does not return.
29: ḥakəmôt śārôtêhā taʿānenāh ʾap-hîʾ tāšîḇ ʾămārêhā lāh — 'Her wisest princesses answer; indeed, she herself answers her own words.' ḥāḵām (H2450, wise) — the 'wise ladies' (feminine plural) are the court women who construct reassuring interpretations of the delay. The phrase ʾap-hîʾ tāšîḇ ʾămārêhā — 'she even answers her own words back to herself' — shows Sisera's mother generating her own comfort rather than receiving it. She speaks in order to silence her own dread. The irony of the wise women's counsel (v30) is that they construct a fiction of victory precisely when the reverse has occurred.
30: hălōʾ yimṣəʾû yəḥallèqû šālāl raḥam raḥămātayim ləraʾš gāḇer — 'Are they not finding and dividing the spoil? A womb or two for every warrior?' The mother's reassuring reconstruction is brutal in its candor: raḥam (H7358, womb — by metonymy, a woman as sexual prize) is the expected spoil of conquest. The Song does not soften this: Sisera's victory would have meant the rape of Israelite women. By naming it plainly, Deborah's Song pronounces judgment on the Canaanite war-economy that commodified women as plunder. The victory that prevented this is thus not merely military but moral — YHWH protected what Sisera would have violated.
